questions.question: Which of the following best describes executive function as defined in the lecture?

options.A: A single localised cognitive process in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex
options.B: An umbrella term for processes that modulate the activity of other cognitive functions
options.C: A motor-control system that initiates voluntary movement
options.D: A long-term memory store for procedural skills

questions.explanation: Executive function is an umbrella term for processes that modulate other cognitive functions in a flexible, goal-directed manner. The professor stressed it plays a supervisory role over the rest of cognition rather than performing one specific function.

questions.question: Which thalamic nucleus serves as the relay station that closes the cortico-striatal-thalamo-cortical loop?
questions.explanation: The mediodorsal thalamic nucleus relays basal ganglia output back to the cortex, making the cortico-striatal-thalamo-cortical circuit a closed loop rather than a one-way street. The professor emphasised the thalamus is often left out of figures but is central to the loop.

questions.question: Which inhibition task is the most sensitive marker for ADHD and depends on the right posterior VLPFC plus basal ganglia?
questions.explanation: The stop-signal task requires withholding a response that has already been initiated, which is more cognitively demanding than pre-emptive inhibition. It is highly sensitive to ADHD and Parkinson's disease and depends on right posterior VLPFC and basal ganglia.

questions.question: In the three parallel basal-ganglia loops, which striatal subregion belongs to the prefrontal/cognitive loop?
questions.explanation: The prefrontal (cognitive executive) loop runs from DLPFC through the anterior caudate. The motor loop uses the putamen and the affective loop uses the ventral striatum, all sharing the same Cortex-Striatum-Pallidum-Thalamus architecture.

questions.question: Brenda Milner's 1963 Wisconsin Card Sorting Test study showed that PFC patients exhibit which behaviour?

options.A: Inability to learn any new sorting rule
options.B: Perseveration — continuing to use a previously valid rule despite negative feedback

questions.explanation: Brenda Milner (1963) reported that patients with PFC damage perseverate — they continue to use the previously valid rule despite repeated negative feedback. The book clarifies they can learn new rules normally if the previous rule is unavailable; the deficit is shifting away from a still-applicable rule.

questions.question: Which ERP component is generated by the oddball task and forms the basis of the Module 5 practical?
questions.explanation: The oddball task generates the P300, a positive ERP deflection beginning around 300 ms post-target. It is robust enough to be picked up with relatively few trials and is the basis of the M5 practical.

questions.question: Which prefrontal subregion is most associated with relating rules, higher-order goals, and exploratory information-seeking behaviour?
questions.explanation: The frontopolar cortex (FPC), the most anterior tip of the PFC, activates specifically for relating rules, tracking goals/subgoals, and exploratory behaviour. Damage particularly impairs complex Progressive Matrices items while simple ones remain intact.

questions.question: What did the lesion study by Fellows & Farah (2005) show that challenges classical accounts of the ACC?

options.A: ACC patients are completely unable to perform the Stroop task
options.B: ACC patients perform normally on Stroop conflict tasks, suggesting ACC may not be strictly necessary
options.C: ACC patients show enhanced conflict resolution
options.D: The ACC is the sole source of the ERN

questions.explanation: Fellows & Farah (2005) showed that patients with ACC damage perform normally on Stroop conflict tasks, challenging the view that ACC is strictly necessary for conflict monitoring. Newer accounts recast ACC activity as detecting mismatch between expectations and outcomes.

questions.question: Which key claim distinguishes Badre & D'Esposito's policy abstraction theory from Koechlin's temporal abstraction theory?

options.A: Policy abstraction holds that posterior PFC supports long-term goals
options.B: Policy abstraction holds that anterior regions implement higher-order policies that determine which simple posterior rule applies — anterior shapes posterior
options.C: Policy abstraction denies any rostro-caudal organisation in the PFC
options.D: Policy abstraction proposes that the cerebellum, not PFC, organises rules

questions.explanation: Badre & D'Esposito's policy abstraction theory holds that anterior PFC implements higher-order policies that determine which simple posterior rule applies (anterior shapes posterior). Koechlin's temporal abstraction theory instead distinguishes posterior current-time rules, middle changing-context rules, and anterior long-term goal rules.

questions.question: What was the central neural finding of the cutting-edge 7T fMRI meditation paper?

options.A: Increased Default Mode Network activity during meditation
options.B: Increased DLPFC activity with no other changes
options.C: Decreased activity in the Default Mode Network during meditation, with no region showing increased activity
options.D: Reduced cerebellar activity only, with no cortical effects

questions.explanation: The 7T fMRI study found reduced activity in the Default Mode Network during focused meditation compared to passive rest, with no brain region showing increased activity. Thalamic and occipital deactivation suggested perceptual decoupling — turning inward away from sensory input.
